chapter: Chapter V — Of Leases of Immovable Property
text: A lease of immoveable property is a transfer of a right to enjoy such property, made for a certain time, express or implied, or in perpetuity, in consideration of a price paid or promised, or of money, a share of crops, service or any other thing of value, to be rendered periodically or on specified occasions to the transferor by the transferee, who accepts the transfer on such terms. The transferor is called the lessor, the transferee is called the lessee, the price is called the premium, and the money, share, service or other thing to be so rendered is called the rent.

section: Section 108 — Rights and Liabilities of Lessor and Lessee
text: In the absence of a contract or local law to the contrary, the lessor and the lessee of immoveable property shall respectively possess the following rights and be subject to the following liabilities: The lessor is bound to disclose to the lessee any material defect in the property; The lessee may deduct repair costs from rent if lessor fails to make necessary repairs; The lessee has right to remove any crops sown by him during the lease. The lessee may not use the property in a manner that destroys or permanently impairs its value. On expiration of lease, lessee must put the lessor into possession of the property.

Notwithstanding any agreement to the contrary, where the standard rent of any premises has been fixed under any previous Rent Act, such standard rent shall be deemed to be the standard rent for the purposes of this Act. Where no standard rent has been fixed, the standard rent shall be determined having regard to the rent at which the premises were last let, the improvements made, the taxes paid, the period and conditions of tenancy. No landlord shall claim or receive rent exceeding the standard rent and permitted increases.

section: Section 4 — Agreement between landlord and tenant
text: No person shall let or take on rent any premises except by an agreement in writing. Every tenancy agreement executed after the commencement of this Act shall be intimated to the Rent Authority by the landlord and the tenant, jointly, within two months from the date of execution of the tenancy agreement. The Rent Authority shall issue a unique identification number to the tenancy agreement.

The landlord shall not collect from the tenant an amount exceeding two months' rent as security deposit in case of residential premises and six months' rent in case of non-residential premises. The security deposit shall be refunded by the landlord to the tenant at the time of vacating the premises after deducting arrears of rent and charges for damages. Where the landlord fails to refund the security deposit, the tenant may apply to the Rent Court for recovery.

A landlord may approach the Rent Court for eviction of tenant on the following grounds: the tenant has neither paid nor tendered the rent due for a period of two months; the premises are required by the landlord for use and occupation of himself or any member of his family; the tenant has created a nuisance or caused damage to the premises; the tenant has sublet the premises without consent of the landlord; the premises are to be demolished or reconstructed. No tenant shall be evicted without an order of the Rent Court.
